coredata.dc:title: Construction of NaYF4:Eu@carbon dots nanocomposites for multifunctional applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Xiaokai
Zhang, Xuejie
Hu, Chaofan
Li, Wei
Lei, Bingfu
Liu, Yingliang
Zhuang, Jianle
coredata.dc:description: The combination of carbon dots (CDs) and rare-earth ions has attracted increasing attention due to their unique fluorescence properties. Herein, CDs are incorporated with NaYF4:Eu to form stable NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites by a simple sol-gel route. It has been demonstrated that CDs are successfully embedded in the silica layer outside NaYF4:Eu nanoparticles. These nanocomposites exhibit characteristic emissions of both CDs and NaYF4:Eu under a single excitation wavelength. By using their dual-emissive properties, the nanocomposites can act as ratiometric fluorescent sensor for selective detection of Fe3+ ions in the range of 0–350 μM. The temperature-dependent luminescent property of the nanocomposites makes them also suitable as thermometers in the range from 377 to 467 K. In addition, the luminescent color can be regularly adjusted by tuning the mass ratio of NaYF4:Eu to CDs. This combing strategy enables us to achieve dual emission of CDs and Eu3+ ions in nanocrystals, opening an avenue towards multifunctional applications in widespread fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NaYF4:Eu
Carbon dots
Nanocomposites
Ratiometric fluorescence
Multifunctional

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showing the preparation and property of NaYF4:Eu@CDs composites.
SEM images of (a) NaYF4:Eu and (b) NaYF4:Eu@CDs. (c) TEM image and (d) HRTEM image of NaYF4:Eu@CDs. (e) TEM image and (f) HRTEM image of CDs.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) FT-IR spectra of CDs, NaYF4:Eu and NaYF4:Eu@CDs. The standard data for NaYF4 (JCPDS card no.06-0342) is shown as a reference.

PL spectra of (a) solid CDs and (b) CDs solution excited at different excitation wavelengths and excitation spectrum of CDs monitored at 453 nm and 454 nm, respectively. The insets of (a) show the optical images of CDs powder under natural light and a 365 nm UV lamp. (c) PL spectra of NaYF4:Eu excited at 395 nm and excitation spectrum of NaYF4:Eu monitored at 612 nm. (d) PL spectra of NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites excited at 395 nm with the photo of NaYF4:Eu@CDs powder inset.

(a) Quenching efficiencies of different concentrations of Fe3+ ions on the fluorescence of NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites under 395 nm excitation. (b) A plot of F460/F612 response to Fe3+ concentration with the range of 0–350 μM (in 10 mg/mL NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites solution) is described. (c) The corresponding CIE chromaticity diagram for those curves in (a).
Effect of Fe3+ ions on the NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites.

(a) PL spectra of NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites recorded every 10 K from 377 to 467 K under 395 nm excitation. (b) The corresponding intensity ratio of NaYF4:Eu (614 nm) to CDs (471 nm) as a function of temperature and the fitted curve for the nanocomposites. (c) The corresponding CIE chromaticity diagram for those curves in (a).

(a) PL spectra of NaYF4:Eu@CDs with different mass ratios: 0:1, 0.5:1, 5:1, 50:1, and 5:0. All samples were excited at 395 nm. (b) CIE chromaticity diagram for CDs, NaYF4:Eu, and NaYF4:Eu@CDs with different mass ratios (samples 1 to 3 refer to mass ratios of 0.5:1, 5:1, 50:1), respectively. All samples were excited at 395 nm. (c) Electroluminescence spectrum of a white LEDs lamp fabricated using a 395 nm chip combined with white-emitting NaYF4:Eu@CDs (mass ratio: 5:1) nanocomposites.

head.ce:title: Construction of NaYF4:Eu@carbon dots nanocomposites for multifunctional applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture, College of Materials and Energy, South China Agricultural University, Guangzhou 510642, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites prepared by a simple sol-gel route can be applied in ion detector, temperature sensing and white LEDs.

The combination of carbon dots (CDs) and rare-earth ions has attracted increasing attention due to their unique fluorescence properties. Herein, CDs are incorporated with NaYF4:Eu to form stable NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites by a simple sol-gel route. It has been demonstrated that CDs are successfully embedded in the silica layer outside NaYF4:Eu nanoparticles. These nanocomposites exhibit characteristic emissions of both CDs and NaYF4:Eu under a single excitation wavelength. By using their dual-emissive properties, the nanocomposites can act as ratiometric fluorescent sensor for selective detection of Fe3+ ions in the range of 0–350 μM. The temperature-dependent luminescent property of the nanocomposites makes them also suitable as thermometers in the range from 377 to 467 K. In addition, the luminescent color can be regularly adjusted by tuning the mass ratio of NaYF4:Eu to CDs. This combing strategy enables us to achieve dual emission of CDs and Eu3+ ions in nanocrystals, opening an avenue towards multifunctional applications in widespread fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), which consist of a new class of fluorescence-based nanoparticles, have drawn increasing attention due to their fascinating merits including unique optical features, nontoxicity and high biocompatibility [1–6]. These properties enable them to find huge applications in the fields of chemical sensing, bioimaging, nanomedicine, photocatalysis, optoelectronic devices, anti-counterfeiting, and so on [7–15]. However, most of the CDs show blue to green emissions and solid-state quenching properties, which restrict their applications in many aspects such as multicolor display and light emitting materials [16]. Moreover, CDs usually show single band emission, the measurements based on which are less convenient and robust than fluorescence ratiometric methods for sensing application, because the ratiometric methods could overcome the inaccuracy caused by instrumental factors and nonuniform distribution of probes through the built-in calibrations of two emission bands [17–19]. Therefore, finding an effective resolution to address these problems still remains as a challenge.

Europium trivalent ion Eu(III) exhibits unique luminescent properties such as narrow red-emission band, long lifetime and high color purity [20–22]. Recently, some research efforts have been devoted to the realization of composites/complexes containing CDs and Eu3+ ions owing to their broad applications especially in sensor technology and multicolor display [23–27]. This is because the composites/complexes can take full use of the components’ advantages and address the problems mentioned above. It was reported that such composites/complexes exhibited dual emission peaks from CDs and Eu3+ ions, which did not interfere with each other and showed different features, making them as potential ideal ratiometric fluorescence sensor [28,29]. To combine CDs and Eu3+ ions, one of the strategies is to coordinate Eu3+ ions to the functional group modified on the surface of CDs. For example, Dong et al. present the synthesis of Eu3+ ions modified CDs via a polyol-mediated synthetic route [30]. Zhou et al. reported the combination of CDs and Eu3+ ions by using diethylenetriamine pentaacetic acid as a chelating ligand [31]. Desai et al. fabricated the Eu3+ hybrid CDs by microwave heating through synergistic effect of EDTA and l-cysteine [32]. However, the emissions of Eu3+ ions in these complexes were usually quenched by water molecules, hindering their widespread applications [33,34]. An improved strategy is to fabricate composites composed of CDs and Eu3+ ions doped inorganic materials. For example, Samanta et al. reported the synthesis and characterization of LaF3:Eu3+-CDs nanocomposites and observed strong luminescence of Eu3+ ions via sensitization by CDs [35]. Our group incorporated CDs and YVO4:Eu3+ nanoparticles within silica networks to construct molecularly imprinted sensor for ratiometric fluorescent detection of paranitrophenol [36]. Very recently, Huang et al. reported the synthesis of CDs@MgWO4:Eu3+ nanosheet which showed increased emission intensity of Eu3+ ions [37]. Despite the previous efforts, the exploration of these composites for multifunctional applications remains in the early stage and needs further study urgently.

In this work, we used NaYF4:Eu combine with CDs to construct nanocomposites by a simple sol-gel route. NaYF4, a well-known luminescent host, has many advantages such as easy control of size and morphology, stable crystal structure and convenient adjustment of spectrum [38–43]. NaYF4:Eu phosphors exhibit good down-conversion luminescent properties, which have been widely applied in solar cells, magneto-optical conversion, bio-imaging and other fields [44–46]. The as-prepared NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites were characterized by SEM, TEM, XRD and FT-IR measurements. It has been proved that CDs were embedded in the silica layer outside the NaYF4:Eu nanoparticles. Interestingly, the nanocomposites can be used as ratiometric fluorescent sensor not only for selective detection of Fe3+ ions but also for temperature sensing in the range from 377 K to 467 K under a single excitation wavelength. Moreover, by tuning the relative mass ratio of CDs and NaYF4:Eu, the luminescent color can be tailored and even white-light emission can be obtained. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report for the construction of NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites for multifunctional applications.

Y(NO3)3 (99.99%), Yb(NO3)3 (99.9%), Eu(NO3)3 (99.99%), (CH2OH)2 (EG, 98%), NH4F (AR), NaCl (AR), C2H5OH (AR) and polyethylenimine (PEI, Mw = 25,000) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl methyldimethoxy silane (AEAPMS), hydrochloric acid, anhydrous citric acid, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), NH3·H2O and epoxide resin were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was used throughout this work.

Sodium chloride (1.538 mmol) was dissolved in 30 mL of ethylene glycol and then 0.6 mL of PEI was added slowly. After stirring for 30 min, 5 mL of solution containing 0.95 mmol of Y3+ and 0.05 mmol of Eu3+ was added into the mixture. Then 4 mmol of ammonium fluoride was poured into the mixture solution. After stirring for another 10 min, the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. The NaYF4:Eu solid particles were centrifuged and washed several times with deionized water and ethanol, and then dried at 60 °C overnight.

The CDs were prepared based on a reported method [48]. Briefly, 10 mL of AEAPMS, 0.4 g of anhydrous citric acid and 10 mL of water were placed into a beaker and stirred for 15 min. The mixture solution was slowly transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After reaction, the autoclaved mixture was cooled to room temperature. The resultant light brown solution was purified through several extractions with ethylacetate and petroleum ether. Finally, the CDs were yielded by rotary evaporation.

For the preparation of the NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites, 0.1 g of NaYF4:Eu nanoparticles were added into 4 mL of silica sol, whose molar ratio of the constituents was TEOS: EtOH:HCl (pH = 2):H2O = 1:4:0.2:5. Then 1 mL of CDs solution was poured into the above mixture solution. The mixture system was kept stirring until uniform gel formed. The derived gel was dried under vacuum at 80 °C for 12 h. The final dried product was ground into powder.

Based on the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, the NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites were used for detect metal ions such as Ag+, Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni+, Zn2+. In a typical detection experiment, the NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites were dispersed in an aqueous solution (10 mg/mL) and various metal ion solutions (400 μM) were added. After mixing evenly, fluorescence measurements were carried out with excitation at 395 nm. In the further experiment, 10 mg/mL NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites solution was prepared and the Fe3+ ions of different concentrations (0–350 μM) were added. Fluorescence measurements were carried out with the same excitation.

In a typical preparation, a mixture of epoxy resin and NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites were obtained by mechanical agitation. The mixture was then carefully integrated on a 395 nm UV-LED chip and the white LEDs was obtained by drying at 60 °C for 4 h.

The FT-IR spectra were recorded on a Nicolet Avatar 360 Fourier transformation infrared spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra were measured on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer. Temperature-dependent PL emission spectra were measured with the same fluorescence spectrometer by combining with a heating apparatus (Oxford instruments). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken on a FEI Tecnai 12 transmission electron microscope and the high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were taken on a JEOL-2100F transmission electron microscope. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded using a Hitachi S-4800 field-emission scanning electron microscope. The XRD patterns were recorded using powder X-ray diffraction (Rigaku-Ultima IV, Japan) with Cu Kα (Kα1 = 0.1541 nm) irradiation at 36 kV and 30 mA with a scanning rate of 8° min−1.

NaYF4:Eu@CDs composites were fabricated by a facile one-step sol-gel method through the hydrolysis of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS). As shown in Fig. 1 , NaYF4:Eu@CDs composites could be obtained after stirring and drying of the mixture containing NaYF4:Eu, CDs and silica sol. The CDs contained a large number of methoxysilane groups, which acted as highly reactive intermediates responsible for combining with hydroxyl and silanol groups. The hydrolysis and condensation of TEOS led to the formation of a silica layer on the surface of NaYF4:Eu nanoparticles. At the same time, CDs were embedded into the silica layer. The composite exhibited blue emission of CDs and red emission of NaYF4:Eu under 395 nm excitation.

The morphology and structure of the as-synthesized NaYF4:Eu, NaYF4:Eu@CDs and CDs were investigated by electron microscopy. Fig. 2 a shows the SEM image of NaYF4:Eu nanoparticles. It is clear that the particle is about 35 nm in size. After combination with CDs, NaYF4:Eu@CDs composites show larger particle size of about 50 nm with uneven surface (Fig. 2b). Typical TEM and HRTEM images of NaYF4:Eu@CDs composites are presented in Fig. 2c and d. It can be found that a silica layer has been deposited on the surface of NaYF4:Eu nanoparticles in which CDs are distributed randomly, confirming the successfully grafting of CDs onto NaYF4:Eu particles. Fig. 2e records the TEM image of the original CDs which indicates the sample is well dispersed with an average size of about 4 nm. The high crystallinity with a lattice fringe of 0.21 nm can be indexed to the interplanar spacing of (1 0 0) planes for graphitic carbon (Fig. 2f).

The crystal structure of CDs, NaYF4:Eu, and NaYF4:Eu@CDs were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. 3 a, for the as-prepared NaYF4:Eu, all the diffraction peaks can be indexed to cubic NaYF4 (JCPDS no. 06-0342). For the NaYF4:Eu@CDs, a hump peak at about 2θ = 20° was found, which could be ascribed to CDs and amorphous SiO2, and the other peaks could be well indexed to cubic NaYF4:Eu, indicating the unchanged of the crystal form for NaYF4:Eu. The FT-IR spectra of CDs, NaYF4:Eu and NaYF4:Eu@CDs are shown in Fig. 3b. For pure NaYF4:Eu, the peaks at 1456 cm−1 and 1570 cm−1 are the characteristic asymmetric vibrations of NH band of the amide group, while the peak at around 2977 cm−1 can be attributed to the vibration of CH2 group. This is due to the presence of hydrophilic PEI on the particle surface. As for CDs, the characteristic peak at 1636 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of the CO groups from the CONH units of CDs. The formation of the silica layer can be confirmed by the presence of the SiOSi asymmetric stretching peak at 1076 cm−1. From the FT-IR spectrum of NaYF4:Eu@CDs, it can be clearly found that both the NH and CO bands exist, as well as the SiOSi asymmetric stretching peak around 1079 cm−1 from the silica layer. These results demonstrate that CDs have been successfully coated on the surface of NaYF4:Eu nanoparticles.

Fig. 4 a shows the PL spectra of the solid CDs at different excitation wavelengths and the strongest emission peak is at 453 nm under excitation at 350 nm. The CDs powder show yellow in color under nature light and blue light under a 365 nm UV lamp (see inset of Fig. 4a). The absence of quenching luminescence in solid state CDs may be due to the formation of a silane coupling agent in the form of the network of CDs. The PL spectra of the CDs solution (1 mg/mL) are similar to that of the CDs powder, as shown in Fig. 4b, which means that CDs exhibit stable luminescent properties in both solid and liquid states. Fig. S1a shows the PL spectra of CDs with different concentrations at 365 nm excitation. It is found clearly that the strongest fluorescence can be obtained when the concentration of CDs is 1 mg/mL. The CDs solution also exhibit blue light under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. S1b).

The characteristic PL spectrum excited at 395 nm and excitation spectrum monitored at 612 nm of solid NaYF4:Eu are shown in Fig. 4c. It can be found that the solid NaYF4:Eu exhibit five typical emission peaks centered at 593 nm, 612 nm, 623 nm, 653 nm and 703 nm, due to the energy level transitions of Eu3+ ions. As expected, the NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites display both the characteristic emissions of CDs and NaYF4:Eu under a single excitation wavelength of 395 nm (Fig. 4d). In addition, the emission of the NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites can be systematically tuned by exciting at different wavelengths. This is due to the fact that the emission peaks of CDs are regularly red-shift while those of NaYF4:Eu are not changed (Fig. S2). The corresponding CIE chromaticity coordinates for NaYF4:Eu@CDs under different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. S3.

It is known that CDs can be used as an effective probe to detect metal ions. Here we investigated the feasibility of using CDs as a fluorescent probe for ion detection. It was found that the fluorescence of CDs solution was obvious quenched by Fe3+ ions, while other ions had less or no effect, suggesting their potential for selective detection of Fe3+ ions (Fig. S4). To illustrate the potential application of our NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites as luminescent ion sensor, we measured the Fe3+-dependent emission spectra of NaYF4:Eu@CDs in the range from 0 to 350 μM with 395 nm excitation (Fig. 5 a). The luminescent intensities of CDs decreased gradually along with the increasing of Fe3+ ion concentration whereas the emissions of NaYF4:Eu were little affected by Fe3+ ions. The intensity ratio of the two emissions is commonly used as the ion concentration parameter in ratiometric luminescent ion sensing. For NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites, this parameter can be defined by using the ratio of the intensity of CDs at 460 nm and that of NaYF4:Eu at 612 nm (F460/F612). The Fe3+ ion concentration dependence of the F460/F612 ratio is plotted in Fig. 5b and a good linearity with a correlation coefficient of 0.99025 is obtained in the range of 0 to 350 μM, which can be expressed as follows:
(1)
F 460
/
F 612
= 1.89779 - 0.12945
C
where C represents the concentration which varies from 0 to 350 μM. In accordance with the CIE chromaticity coordinates in Fig. 5c, the color footprints pass from blue to white light region. This result indicates that the nanocomposites can be used as a reliable and useful ion probe. It should be noted that although many luminescent probes have been developed for Fe3+ detection, most of them are based on the fluorescence quenching (turn-off) detection. Because the fluorescence intensity can be severely affected by any changes in probe concentration, excitation or detection efficiency, fluorescence ratiometric methods can eliminate most of the blur by employing a ratio of the two. Therefore, the probe based on the dual-emissive NaYF4:Eu@CDs will have great advantages in practical applications. The comparisons of the analytical performance of the present method with the reported methods for the detection of Fe3+ ion are listed in Table S1.

The mechanism diagram showing the effect of Fe3+ ions on the NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites is elucidated in Fig. 6 . There are a large number of peptide bonds (CONH) on the surface of CDs in the composites which can interact with Fe3+ ions to form complex. The complex can change the electronic structure of CDs and affect the distribution of excitons, which facilitates the non-radiative recombination of the excitons through charge or energy transfer process and results in the fluorescence quenching [47].

The dual emission property of NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposite also makes it a possible excellent temperature sensor because these two emissions corresponding to CDs and NaYF4:Eu have different temperature sensitivity. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the PL spectra of NaYF4:Eu@CDs are provided from 377 to 467 K with an interval of 10 K at the excitation of 395 nm. The luminescent intensities of both CDs at 471 nm and NaYF4:Eu at 614 nm decrease gradually with increasing temperature. This can be ascribed to the fact that the non-radiative recombination was enhanced with increasing temperature, leading to the PL thermal quenching [18]. As the temperature increases, the emission peak at 614 nm decreases slightly, while that at 471 nm decreases obviously. The thermometric parameter is defined by the ratio of the emission intensity of NaYF4:Eu at 614 nm to that of CDs at 471 nm (F614/F471). As shown in Fig. 7b, a good linear relationship with a correlation coefficient of 0.9887 over the range from 377 to 467 K is obtained, which can be expressed as follows:
(2)
F 614
/
F 471
= 0.05764 T - 20.935
where T represents the temperature in the range of 377 to 467 K. According to the CIE chromaticity coordinates in Fig. 7c, the color footprints run across the white light region to red orange light. The results suggested that we have successfully used sample bimodal to build ratio temperature sensors.

The PL spectra of NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites with different relative concentrations of NaYF4:Eu and CDs (mass ratios: 0:1, 0.5:1, 5:1, 50:1, and 5:0) under 395 nm excitation are shown in Fig. 8 a. It can be found that the luminescent intensity of Eu3+ ions gradually increases with increasing concentration of NaYF4:Eu. From the PL spectra, one can see clearly that the proportion of red emission increases significantly compared to blue emission. Thus, the luminescent colors of the NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites can be regulated from blue to red in line with different proportions of red and blue emissions. Fig. 8b shows the corresponding CIE chromaticity coordinates of different NaYF4:Eu@CDs samples under 395 nm excitation. The five color dots represent the pure CDs, the NaYF4:Eu@CDs from 1 to 3 with different relative concentrations (mass ratio: 0.5:1, 5:1, 50:1) and pure NaYF4:Eu, respectively. The color can be tuned systematically from blue to white and to red. The corresponding photographs are shown in the upper right of Fig. 8b. It suggests that the color composition could be regulated by changing the mass ratio of NaYF4:Eu to CDs to meet specific requirements. The optical property indicates that NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites may have a great potential in the fabrication of white LEDs. Then we combined the NaYF4:Eu@CDs sample (mass ratio: 5:1) and a light-emitting chip (395 nm) to fabricate a white LEDs, as shown in Fig. 8c. This device gives an intense white light with CIE coordinates of (0.293, 0.281). It is concluded that our NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites are promising candidates for practical lighting applications.

In summary, we successfully fabricated NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites by a simple sol-gel route through the hydrolysis of TEOS. CDs are demonstrated to embed in the silica layer coating on the surface of NaYF4:Eu nanoparticles. Compared with the previous methods for combining CDs and Eu3+ ions, this strategy endow the composites more stable structure and PL property [23–26]. The NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites exhibit dual-emission properties which correspond to the emissions of CDs and Eu3+ ions, respectively. By using the fascinating dual-emission properties, these nanocomposites are used to construct ratiometric fluorescent probe for ion detection and temperature sensor, which could show higher accuracy than the reported fluorescent quenching route [2,49–51]. Moreover, the emission of NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites can be tuned by optimizing their relative mass ratios and the nanocomposites show great potential for multicolor displays and white LEDs. This is the first report for the construction of NaYF4:Eu@CDs nanocomposites for multifunctional applications. It is expected that the development of such composites combining rare-earth luminescent materials and CDs will find more applications in many areas.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51602108), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (2017A030313256 and 2014A030313466), and the Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Materials Chemistry and Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (2008DP173016).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2019.02.038.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
